coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot/g-C3N4-mediated self-activated antimicrobial nanocomposite films for active packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Dezhuang
Zhang, Xinhua
Li, Jiaxu
Li, Yingying
Guo, Jialiang
Zhang, Xiuling
Zhang, Wentao
coredata.dc:description: A novel carbon dot/g-C3N4 nanocomposite (CCN) exhibiting enhanced photocatalytic activity was developed and used as a photoactive nanofiller to construct corn starch/carboxymethyl cellulose (CS/CMC)-based functional films. The morphologies and structures of the CCN-CS/CMC composite films were investigated with scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction. The effects of the CCN on the physicochemical properties and antibacterial activities of the films were analyzed. The properties of the films were optimized with the addition of CCN (0.20 mg/mL), and the tensile strength of the film was increased to 11.9 MPa and the water contact angle was increased to 103.39°. The optimal active film showed > 99.9 % antibacterial efficiencies against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus under visible light and prolonged the shelf lives of bananas for more than four days compared to the 4-day shelf life of the control. This work provides a novel route for developing antimicrobial active packaging.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot/g-C3N4-mediated self-activated antimicrobial nanocomposite films for active packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150030, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150030, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Changchun Normal University, Changchun, Jilin 130032, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang Province, 150030, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbon dot/g-C3N4 nanocomposite (CCN) exhibiting enhanced photocatalytic activity was developed and used as a photoactive nanofiller to construct corn starch/carboxymethyl cellulose (CS/CMC)-based functional films. The morphologies and structures of the CCN-CS/CMC composite films were investigated with scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction. The effects of the CCN on the physicochemical properties and antibacterial activities of the films were analyzed. The properties of the films were optimized with the addition of CCN (0.20 mg/mL), and the tensile strength of the film was increased to 11.9 MPa and the water contact angle was increased to 103.39°. The optimal active film showed > 99.9 % antibacterial efficiencies against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus under visible light and prolonged the shelf lives of bananas for more than four days compared to the 4-day shelf life of the control. This work provides a novel route for developing antimicrobial active packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Food spoilage caused by foodborne pathogens has emerged as a threat to the quality and safety of food and human health (Duan, Li, Meng, Wang, & Wu, 2021). To prevent food spoilage and maintain food quality, food packaging films with antimicrobial activity have attracted tremendous attention. Many biopolymers, such as corn starch (CS), pectin (CP), and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), have been commonly researched in the packaging field due to their biodegradability, sustainability, and film-forming properties (Lan et al., 2021; Nikolic, Vasiljevic, Auger, & Vidic, 2021). Among them, CS has the advantages of abundance and renewability and is a promising candidate for the development of environmentally friendly packaging. Recent studies have shown that blending other biopolymers into the matrix of CS can improve the physical properties of the blended films (Chen et al., 2022; Tavares et al., 2020). Tavares et al. (2020) reported that blended CS films with CMC increased mechanical and water vapor barrier properties.

Recently, various antibacterial active substances (such as natural extracts) have been incorporated into the biopolymer matrix to provide CS/CMC-based films with antibacterial properties (Chen et al., 2022; Hou, Wang, Shi, & Yue, 2023; Lan et al., 2021). However, considering possible decreases in antibacterial activity over time and the potential for bacterial resistance, further development of CS/CMC-based active films with sustained antimicrobial activity is still necessary. On the one hand, food is fresh at the beginning of preservation, and even packaging films with weak antibacterial activity can prevent the growth of bacteria. However, packaged foods are more easily infected by pathogenic bacteria with extended storage times; thus, the demand for packaging with antibacterial activity is increasing. Conventional packaging films provide strong antibacterial activity in the early stages of food storage but become weaker in the later stages due to continuous consumption of the active substances (Ni et al., 2021). On the other hand, some antimicrobial agents may also easily induce bacterial resistance during long-term service. These factors will reduce the persistent antimicrobial efficiency of active substances. Therefore, developing novel packaging films with responsive and persistent antimicrobial functions is imperative.

Photodynamic antibacterial therapy (PAT) is an advanced technology that uses photosensitizers and appropriate excitation sources to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can oxidatively damage surrounding biomolecules, such as lipids, functional proteins and nucleic acids, and thus inhibit bacteria (Pang, Li, Zhu, Cheng, & Liu, 2020). In comparison to other antimicrobials, PAT can not only inhibit bacteria but also effectively prevents the occurrence of bacterial resistance because it does not require specific targeting interactions between photosensitizers and bacteria (Jia, Song, Li, & Huang, 2019). Combining photosensitizer-doped packaging films with PAT is expected to result in responsive and persistent antibacterial performance. As a nontoxic metal-free photocatalytic material, graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) can be incorporated into a CS/CMC-based nanocomposite film as a photosensitizer to achieve the photodynamic inactivation of foodborne pathogens under visible light. Unfortunately, pristine g-C3N4 suffers from a weak visible-light response and rapid electron–hole recombination, thus requiring long-term illumination, which may lead to nutrient loss in food (Ni, Sun, & Wang, 2021). Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of zero-dimensional nanocarbon material with unique optical properties and nontoxicity that have been shown to enhance the photoactivity of g-C3N4 (Wang et al., 2018). Our previous studies have confirmed that citrus peel-derived CDs (CPCDs) can reduce the band gap width of g-C3N4-based nanocomposites and effectively prevent the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers, thereby improving photocatalytic performance (Gong et al., 2023).

In this work, metal-free carbon dot/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (CCNs) with enhanced photoactivity were prepared by depositing citrus peel-derived CDs (CPCDs) onto g-C3N4. Then, the synthesized CCNs were incorporated into a CS/CMC-based biopolymer matrix as photoactive nanofillers at different ratios to obtain a series of controllable functional films with visible-light-driven antibacterial activity. The aim of this study was to examine the effect of CCN addition on the morphology, structure, physicochemical properties and visible-light-driven antibacterial efficacy of the CS/CMC films and clarify the visible-light-driven antibacterial mechanism of the CCN-CS/CMC film. Furthermore, the preservation ability and biosafety of the prepared nanocomposite films were verified. Compared with conventional packaging films, the photodynamic composite film used in this study meets the requirement of increasing the demand of functional activity as storage time increases. Our developed CCN-based visible light responsive nanocomposite films have great potential in food fresh-keeping.

Urea, corn starch (98 % purity) and carboxymethyl cellulose (viscosity: 300–800, degree of substitution: 0.7) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycerin, isopropanol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate dihydrate (EDTA-2Na), anhydrous sodium sulfite (ASS) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyethylene (PE) films, citrus peel and fresh banana were obtained from a local market in Harbin, China.

A simple and green one-step hydrothermal method was used to prepare citrus peel-derived carbon dots (CPCDs) following a previously reported procedure (Gong et al., 2023). Specifically, citrus peels were dried at 45 °C for 15 h, and then powder was prepared with the help of a grinder mixer. The dried and pounded citrus peel powder (2.0 g) was ultrasonically (450 W, 10 min) treated in 60 mL of deionized (DI) water and then subjected to magnetic stirring for 20 min. The resultant mixture was sealed in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling, the brownish-black suspension was centrifuged at 6000 rpm and filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. The obtained brown aqueous solution was freeze-dried to acquire CPCDs.

g-C3N4 (CN) was synthesized using urea as the precursor following a previously reported procedure (Gong et al., 2023). Briefly, 20 g of urea was placed in a covered crucible and heated at 550 °C for 3 h. After cooling, the as-obtained bulk samples were ground into a yellowish powder (g-C3N4) for further use. Next, 0.5 g of g-C3N4 and 0.1 g of CPCDs were dispersed in 50 mL of DI water and ultrasonically treated for 120 min, and the resulting suspension of brownish-yellow particles was placed in a heated Teflon-lined autoclave at 120 °C for 4 h. After centrifugation, the target citrus peel-derived carbon dot/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were obtained and labeled CCNs.

The morphologies of the CPCDs, g-C3N4, and CCNs were preliminarily observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100Plus, Japan). The chemical properties and elemental composition of the CCNs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250XI, USA) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Hitachi SU8010, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using an RF-6000 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

First, the biosafety of the as-developed nanocomposites was assessed by performing cell cytotoxicity. L929 cells (purchased from American Type Culture Collection, ATCC) were cultured in minimum essential medium (MEM) supplemented with 10 % fetal calf serum (FCS), 100 U/mL penicillin, 100 µg/mL streptomycin and 4 mmol/L l-glutamine in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. The CCN samples (10, 15, 20, 25 and 50 mg) were ultrasonically (450 W, 10 min) dispersed in 10 mL of MEM until blended. L929 cells (90 µL) were seeded in 96-well plates and treated with 10 µL of CCN mixture for 24 h. A Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was applied to analyze cell viability according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Second, hemolysis assays were used to further verify biosafety according to a previously described method (Ni et al., 2021). In brief, the CCN samples (10, 20 and 50 mg) were ultrasonically (450 W, 10 min) dispersed in normal saline (100 mL) for further use. Human blood (2.0 mL) was collected and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. After, the blood cells (0.2 mL) were taken and mixed with normal saline (1.0 mL) and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Cell suspensions were prepared by removing the supernatant and adding 0.25 mL of normal saline. Then, 1.0 mL of different concentrations of CCN samples (0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 mg/mL) were separately mixed with 20 μL of the above cell suspension and kept at 37 °C for 4 h. The mixed solution was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 5 min, and the absorbance of each supernatant was measured at 545 nm. Normal saline (1.0 mL) was used as a negative control group, and deionized water (1.0 mL) was used as a positive control group. The relative hemolysis rate was calculated by the following equation: Relative hemolysis (%) = (As – Anc)/(Apc – Anc) × 100 %, where As, Anc and Apc are the absorbance values of the sample, the negative control and the positive control, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CS/CMC-based film-forming solution (CFS) and composite films
ce:section.ce:para: First, 4.5 g of CS, 0.5 g of CMC, and 1.75 g of glycerin (plasticizer) were successively added to a 100 mL colloidal aqueous solution of CCN at different concentrations (0, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, and 0.25 mg/mL); then, the mixtures were heated at 85 °C and stirred for 30 min to form paste solutions. Each mixture was then degassed in a vacuum to eliminate bubbles, and the film-forming solution (FS) was obtained. Second, 15 mL of the FS was cast onto plastic Petri dishes (9.0 cm in diameter) and dried in an oven at 50 °C, resulting in the final samples, which were designated CS/CMC, CCN0.10-CS/CMC, CCN0.15-CS/CMC, CCN0.20-CS/CMC, and CCN0.25-CS/CMC nanocomposite films. After, all film samples were peeled and conditioned at 25 °C in 50 % relative humidity for 48 h. Methods for characterizing the film properties are presented in the Supplementary Material.

The rheological properties of the CS/CMC-based film-forming solution (CFS) were evaluated via a HAAKE MARS60 rotational rheometer (Thermo Fisher, Germany) equipped with a plate-plate geometry (with a diameter of 20 mm). The flow curves of the CFS were determined over the shear rate range of 0.1–100 s−1. The storage and loss moduli of the CFS were recorded by performing the frequency sweep test at a constant strain value of 1 % (determined by a strain sweep experiment) in the range of 0.1–10 Hz.

Escherichia coli (gram-negative) and Staphylococcus aureus (gram-positive) were selected as typical target foodborne pathogens to explore the visible-light-driven antibacterial activity of the CCN-CS/CMC composite films by studying the inhibition zones and antibacterial efficiency of the films. The test bacteria were aseptically inoculated into lysogeny broth (LB) medium (20 mL) and cultured in a shaker at 37 °C for 16 h. Then, E. coli and S. aureus suspensions with a concentration of 1.0 × 106 CFU/mL were obtained by diluting the bacterial solution. All experimental utensils were sterilized using autoclaves (121 °C, 30 min), and the test film samples were irradiated under an ultraviolet lamp for 30 min. For the antibacterial efficiency experiment, various circular films (CS/CMC, CCN0.10-CS/CMC, CCN0.15-CS/CMC, CCN0.20-CS/CMC, and CCN0.25-CS/CMC) with a diameter of ∼ 8 mm were placed in the corresponding holes of a 48-well plate. Then, 0.3 mL bacterial suspension was added into each hole containing the film samples, which were then allowed to interact for 60 min in the absence and presence of visible light irradiation (300 W Xenon lamp). The bacterial suspensions without film and treated under the same conditions were set as controls. After reacting for 60 min, 0.1 mL of the above bacterial suspension was serially diluted, inoculated on LB agar plates and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h, and the resulting bacterial colonies were counted. The relative survival rate (RSR) was calculated by the formula RSR (%) = Ne/N0 × 100 % (Ne and N0 stand for the number of bacterial colonies in the control and treatment groups, respectively). For the inhibition zone assay, sterile film samples were carefully attached to the surface of LB agar plates coated with a bacterial suspension and exposed to visible light/darkness for 60 min. Subsequently, the assembled plate samples were cultured for 24 h at 37 °C, and the diameter of the inhibition zone was measured.

The type of functional ROS generated by the CCN-CS/CMC composite films during the visible-light-driven antibacterial process was determined by free radical-trapping experiments. Disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate dihydrate (EDTA-2Na), isopropanol (IPA), anhydrous sodium sulfite (ASS), and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) were used as hole (h+), hydroxyl radical (•OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and superoxide radical (O2·-) quenchers, respectively. Following the inhibition zone experimental procedure, the four quenchers were individually added around the film samples before being irradiated with visible light. Subsequently, the generation of reactive species was proven by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker EMX Plus, Germany) tests using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the electron spin-trapping agent.

Among all the prepared CCN-CS/CMC composite films, the CCN0.20-CS/CMC films possessed optimal comprehensive performance and desirable visible-light-driven bactericidal efficacy; therefore, this type of film was selected as a representative film to perform packaging tests. Bananas with similar ripeness and quality were equally allocated into four groups and packaged with PE films, pure CS/CMC films, and CCN0.20-CS/CMC films; bananas with no treatment were used as a control. All banana samples were then stored at 20 °C and 75 % RH, followed by exposure to visible light for 60 min at 2-day intervals. Typical pictures and the color change, weight loss, and firmness of the bananas before and after storage were obtained and recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CCNs
ce:section.ce:para: A green hydrothermal self-assembly method was used to prepare innocuous nonmetallic nanocomposites with highly efficient photodynamic sterilization properties based on a previous study by our lab (Gong et al., 2023). The color of the synthesized CCN sample changed from yellow to brownish-yellow compared to that of the pure CN (Fig. S1). The morphologies of the CPCDs, CN and resultant CCN were observed by TEM (Fig. 1 a-d). The TEM and high-resolution TEM images shown in Fig. 1(a-b) reveal the quasispherical nanotopography of the CPCDs. The size of the CPCDs was approximately 3 ∼ 6 nm. The TEM image of g-C3N4 shown in Fig. 1c indicates that g-C3N4 possessed a flat laminated nanoflake structure. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 1d, with the red rings representing the CPCDs, the dispersed CPCDs were randomly anchored onto the CN nanosheets. In addition, the energy dispersive spectra of pure g-C3N4 and the CCNs are displayed in Fig. S2a. Compared with that in the original CN, the carbon and oxygen content in the CCN sample was significantly increased, which further highlighted the successful introduction of CPCDs.

The chemical structure and elemental composition of the synthesized CN and CCN were further investigated by XPS measurements. The XPS survey spectra showed that the CN and CCN samples were mainly composed of C, N, and O elements (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s are shown in Fig. 2(b-d). The C 1 s spectrum of pure CN shown in Fig. 2b was divided into three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.3 eV, corresponding to adventitious carbon, CO bonds and graphite sp2-bonded carbon (N-CN), respectively (Hu et al., 2022). Of note is that the C 1 s spectrum of the CCNs showed that the characteristic peak corresponding to CO bonds shifted to 287.2 eV, which provided reliable evidence for the occurrence of COC bond formation during the synthesis of the CCNs (Chen et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 2c, the N 1s spectra of both CN and CCNs were deconvoluted into four distinct peaks: the three peaks located at 398.7, 400.1 and 401.1 eV were attributed to the sp2-N (C-NC), tertiary N (N-(C)3) and NH groups in CN, and another weak broad peak at approximately 404.6 eV was attributed to the charging effects of π-excitations (Wang et al., 2019). Next, the O 1s spectrum was resolved into three binding energy peaks (Fig. 2d). The O 1s peak at 532.6 eV was indicative of chemisorbed oxygen (surface hydroxyl OH), while another two peaks appeared at 532.4 and 533.7 eV and were attributed to the epoxide structure (CO) and COC groups in the CCNs, respectively (Wang et al., 2017). The TEM, EDS and XPS results mentioned above confirmed the successful preparation of the CCNs. Moreover, the proposed chemical structure for the synthesized nanocomposite is shown in Fig. S3.

To verify the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared CCNs, the separation/transfer process of photogenerated carriers in photocatalysts was examined using PL spectral measurements. A weaker PL emission peak intensity indicated inhibited photocarrier recombination and superior photocatalytic performance (Wang et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. S2b, pure g-C3N4 and CCNs exhibited intrinsic PL emission bands at 440 nm and 447 nm, respectively, with excitation at 370 nm. The PL emission peak intensity of the CCNs was much lower than that of bare CN, which showed that the combination of CPCDs and CN suppressed the radiative recombination of photocarriers and promoted the charge separation efficiency. Notably, a remarkable redshift was observed in the PL spectra of the CCNs, owing to the interaction between the CPCDs and CN. Combined with our previous studies, N atoms with strong electron affinity in CPCDs could be used as electron traps for electron capture to prevent the recombination of the electron-hole pairs (Gong et al., 2023). These results confirmed that the as-developed CCNs had a higher photocarrier separation efficiency, resulting in significantly enhanced photocatalytic reactivity (Wang et al., 2018).

The rheological properties of the FS played a significant role in the casting process of nanocomposite films. The flow behavior of the CFSs was investigated and is shown in Fig. S4. As shown in Fig. S4a, all CFSs demonstrated non-Newtonian behavior (pseudoplastic property) as the apparent viscosity (η) decreased as a function of shear rate (ɣ̇) (Wang et al., 2022). With the introduction of CCNs, the η of the CFS increased, which could be attributed to the intermolecular interactions (hydrogen bonds) between the CCNs and the polymer side chains; this was similar to that previously reported for the starch-based FS (Romero-Bastida et al., 2018). In particular, the CCN0.20-CS/CMC FS displayed the highest apparent viscosity, which indicated that it possessed the most cohesive interlinked network; therefore, a better film-forming capacity was achieved. Subsequently, the dynamic rheology of these CFSs was further studied to evaluate their viscoelastic properties. The storage modulus (G′) reflects solid-like properties of the FS, and the loss modulus (G′′) reflects liquid-like properties. The frequency-dependent flow curves of all CFSs at a strain of 1 % are shown in Fig. S4 (b-c). In all cases, both G′ and G′′ values exhibited a slight dependence on frequency, and G′ was always higher than G′′, suggesting that the FSs were primarily elastic (Romero-Bastida et al., 2018). Among all the CFSs, the obtained CCN0.20-CS/CMC FS showed the highest dynamic moduli, which implied that it had the most pronounced gel-like behavior and the strongest intermolecular interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CS/CMC film and CCN-CS/CMC composite films
Surface color of the CCN-CS/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para: CS/CMC-based composite films containing different amounts of CCNs were synthesized through a solvent-casting method. The pictorial representation of all the prepared films is given in Fig. 3 a. Both the pure CS/CMC film and CCN-CS/CMC composite films were free-standing and flexible and could be repeatedly folded, rolled and bent without breaking (Fig. S5). The CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite film samples maintained a structured shape and relatively high transparency after the addition of CCNs, indicating that the dispersion of CCNs in the blend film was uniform Photographs of the Northeast Agricultural University badges covered with transparent films could be observed in Fig. 3a, which emphasized the excellent transparency of the composite films. In addition, the color of the CS/CMC-based films should not be ignored when determining their practical application and acceptability. The CCN-containing film samples were pale yellow and darkened with increasing CCN concentration. To evaluate the surface color of the CS/CMC-based blend films, their L*, a*, b*, and ΔE* values were further determined (Table S1). As the CCN concentration increased from 0.10 to 0.25 mg/mL, the L* value gradually decreased, while the a* and b* values increased. Accordingly, the ΔE* values of the composite films increased. These changes indicated that the excessive addition of the CCNs reduced the transparency of the CS/CMC-based nanocomposite films, potentially due to the inherent brownish-yellow color of the CCNs. Riahi, Rhim, Bagheri, Pircheraghi and Lotfali (2022) reported similar color changes in carboxymethyl cellulose/CQD composite films.

The surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the prepared CS/CMC film and CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite films were observed by SEM (Fig. 3(b-c)). Evidently, the neat CS/CMC film exhibited a homogenous but somewhat irregular surface, which was attributed to the incomplete interaction between the biopolymers (Tavares et al., 2020). Incorporating CCNs caused the microstructure of the composite film to be more compact with a coarser surface. Nevertheless, no apparent voids or cracks were observed, which indicated superior compatibility between the CCNs and the CS/CMC biopolymer substrate as the CCN concentration was increased to 0.20 mg/mL. The compact and uniform morphology was attributed to the interactions between the CCNs and the CS/CMC biopolymer substrate as well as the filling of nanosized CCNs in the interspace of the films (Ni et al., 2021). The homogeneous dispersion of the CCNs in the CS/CMC-based film matrix also promoted the interfacial interaction between the components (Rammak, Boonsuk, & Kaewtatip, 2021). As the CCN concentration increased to 0.25 mg/mL, the CCN-CS/CMC film sample displayed a fractured and rough surface structure due to the aggregation of the CCNs. Similar results were also found on the surface of starch-based composite films loaded with ZnO-chitosan nanoparticles (Hu et al., 2019). Regarding the cross-section morphology, some cracks and pores were observed for the pure CS/CMC film, while the CCN-CS/CMC blended films containing 0.20 mg/mL CCNs were uniform and dense. This observation was in agreement with the surface morphology results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure of the CCN-CS/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structures of the prepared films and the possible intermolecular interactions between the CCNs and CS/CMC film matrix were studied with the help of FTIR spectroscopy and XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. S6a, the FTIR spectra of all the prepared films displayed a broad absorption peak at approximately 2927 cm−1 corresponding to the CH stretching vibrations, while the band at 1650 cm−1 was related to the bound-water bending vibrations (Zhu et al., 2021). The discernible peaks at approximately 998–1150 cm−1 were assigned to the characteristic stretching modes of the CO pyranoid ring, COC glycosidic bond and COH groups (Chu et al., 2022). The spectrum of the neat CS/CMC film showed a broad absorption peak at 3290 cm−1 ascribed to the OH stretching vibration. However, this peak showed a slight shift toward higher wavelengths for the CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite films after the incorporation of CCNs. This result indicated the formation of hydrogen bond interactions between the CCNs and the polymer matrix (Tavares, de Campos, Mitsuyuki, Luchesi, & Marconcini, 2019). Moreover, with increasing CCN content, a weak band gradually appeared at approximately 1601 cm−1, which could be associated with the characteristic stretching of the aromatic heterocycles in the CCNs (Chen et al., 2022). Fig. S6b shows the XRD patterns of the neat CS/CMC film and CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite films with various CCN incorporations. Compared with that of the pure CS/CMC film, the amorphous peak of the CCN-decorated films shifted from 19.6° to 20.2°, which was direct evidence of the interlayer spacing change, thus reflecting the occurrence of interfacial interactions between the CCNs and polymer matrix (Wang et al., 2019; Kowalczyk et al., 2021). No additional diffraction peak was observed for the CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite films, which indicated that the addition of CCNs did not affect the crystalline integrity of the nanocomposite films (Kowalczyk et al., 2021). The FTIR and XRD results suggested the existence of strong interactions between the constituents in the CS/CMC-based nanocomposite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of the CCN-CS/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal properties of the neat CS/CMC film and CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite films were initially studied by thermogravimetric (TG) analysis and analyzed with derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) curves. As shown in Fig. S6(c-d), all five blended films exhibited three main degradation stages during thermal decomposition. Based on the TG and DTG curves, it was observed that the maximum decomposition temperatures (Tmax values) of the pure CS/CMC, CCN0.10-CS/CMC, CCN0.15-CS/CMC, CCN0.20-CS/CMC and CCN0.25-CS/CMC composite films were 278.3 °C, 281.4 °C, 278.8 °C, 282.4 °C and 280.4 °C, respectively. Additionally, the corresponding char residues of these films were 14.0 %, 15.6 %, 16.5 %, 17.4 % and 16.8 % after thermal decomposition at 600 °C. It was obvious that the CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite films showed higher Tmax values and higher residual ratios than the neat CS/CMC film, indicating that the thermal stability of composite films increased when the CCNs were added. A similar improvement in film thermal properties was reported in previous studies (Duan, Li, Meng, Wang, & Wu, 2021; Rammak, Boonsuk, & Kaewtatip, 2021). The improvement in thermal stability likely occurred because the CCNs, as physically active sites, promoted cross-linking between the film components to form a dense network structure, thus providing a heat absorption barrier for the composite film. Additionally, the uniform dispersion of the thermally stable CCNs in the composite films hindered thermal transport through the polymer film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness and mechanical properties of the CCN-CS/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para: The film thickness exhibited a close relationship with various physical properties. The thickness (μm) of the composite films produced by CS/CMC and using CCNs at different concentrations (0.10, 0.15, 0.20, and 0.25 mg/mL) is shown in Fig. S7a. The thickness of the neat CS/CMC film was approximately 113.8 μm, which is generally in agreement with the SEM results. As the CCNs were incorporated into the pure CS/CMC film, a significant decrease in the thickness was observed. This potentially occurred because the incorporation of CCN in the CS/CMC-based films caused more intermolecular hydrogen bonds. This finding was similar to that of Babaei-Ghazvini, Shahabi-Ghahfarrokhi, and Goudarzi (2018), who reported a thickness reduction with the addition of ZnO for starch/kefiran-based composite films. Several previous studies have reported constant or increased film thickness. This phenomenon indicated that the thickness change of the film depended on the type of polymer matrix and nanofiller. The type of hydrogen bond in the complex could affect the film thickness. The presence of CCNs also resulted in a greater density for the composite films. This could be because a new interfacial adhesion was formed between the CCNs and CS/CMC, which could promote the reduction in the free volume of the polymer matrix; thus, greater compactness was achieved. The tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) of the prepared CS/CMC-based films were determined (Fig. S7b). The TS was gradually increased with increasing CCN concentration up to 0.20 mg/mL but dropped at 0.25 mg/mL CCN. This could be attributed to the following: First, the nanocomposite films have good interfacial surface adhesion, and there are strong molecular interactions between the uniformly dispersed CCNs with high specific surface areas and the CS/CMC polymer matrix. Second, the nanofillers were compatible with the blended polymer matrix and formed a stable reinforced network structure in the CCN-decorated nanocomposite films. However, the excessive addition of CCNs led to the reverse change in TS, which could be related to the weakened interfacial surface adhesion between the CCNs and the polymer matrix (Hu, Jia, Zhi, Jin, & Miao, 2019). Many COH groups were introduced with the excessive incorporation of CCN, which increased the polarity and the length of the branched chains, resulting in a reduction in TS. On the other hand, the EB of CS/CMC-based films significantly decreased after the addition of CCNs, with the CCN-CS/CMC film containing 0.25 mg/mL CCN showing the lowest EB of 53.5 %. Two possible factors for this EB reduction are: 1) increased film rigidity and 2) restricted molecular chain motion initiated by the filling of the free volume between the polysaccharide chains. Similar results were found by Dash, Ali, Das and Mohanta (2019), who reported that adding TiO2 nanoparticles led to a gradual decrease in the EB of starch/pectin-based composite films. Overall, the incorporation of CCNs enhanced the mechanical properties of the nanocomposite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water resistance and barrier properties of CCN-CS/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para: Water resistance and barrier properties are usually crucial characteristics for the practical application of packaging films. Fig. S7c shows the moisture content (MC), swelling ratio (SR), and water solubility (WS) of the neat CS/CMC film and composite films decorated with CCNs. For the pure CS/CMC film, the MC, SR and WS values were 6.68 %, 348 % and 29.72 %, respectively. The addition of CCNs reduced the water sensitivity of the films, resulting in a decrease in MC, SR and WS to 4.15 %, 259 % and 20.31 %, respectively, at the highest CCN concentration. This was probably related to hydrogen cross-linking between the CCNs and the polymer matrix and the resultant increase in the compactness of the network structure of the film (Duan et al., 2022).

The surface wettability of all CS/CM-based composite films as measured by the water contact angle (WCA) is presented in Fig. S7d. The neat CS/CMC film exhibited a relatively low WCA of 83.70°, indicating that it possessed a slightly hydrophilic surface. By incorporating CCNs, the hydrophobicity of the CCN-CS/CMC films increased, the WCA increased to different degrees, and the water resistance was markedly enhanced. At a CCN concentration of 0.20 mg/mL, the maximum WCA of the composite film reached 103.39°. The significant increase in the WCA was caused by the intermolecular interactions between CCNs and film constituents promoting the reorientation of molecules; thus, the cohesion of the composite film increased. This could reduce the content of hydrophilic groups, such as free hydroxyl groups, in the CCN-CS/CMC films and restrict the interactions between the CCN-CS/CMC films and water molecules (Hu et al., 2022; Peighambardoust, Peighambardoust, Pournasir, & Pakdel, 2019). In addition, the relatively high surface roughness of the CCN-CS/CMC composite film contributed to the increased tension of the water droplets, which resulted in a higher WCA; hence, improved hydrophobicity was achieved. These results were corroborated by the intermolecular interactions, such as hydrogen bonds formed between the CCNs and the polymer matrix, in the film observed from the FTIR analysis and the coarser surface of CCN-CS/CMC films observed from SEM images and further supported by previous studies (Jiang, Zhang, Gao, Li, & Hou, 2022). As mentioned above, the water resistance of the CS/CMC-based composite films was effectively improved after adding CCNs, which was more instrumental for the packaging application of the composite films.

Considering that the water vapor permeability (WVP) is an essential parameter of packaging films since it reflects the water vapor barrier property of the film, the WVP of the pure CS/CMC film and as-developed CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite films were tested and are summarized in Fig. S7e. With the introduction of CCNs, the WVP of CS/CMC-based composite films decreased from ∼ 5.75 × 10-7 g·m−1·h−1·Pa−1 to ∼ 5.52 × 10-7 g·m−1·h−1·Pa−1, ∼5.16 × 10-7 g·m−1·h−1·Pa−1, ∼5.00 × 10-7 g·m−1·h−1·Pa−1, and ∼ 4.91 × 10-7 g·m−1·h−1·Pa−1. This result showed that the presence of CCN in the composite film matrix hindered the permeation of the water vapor molecules through the films. This potentially occurred because the uniform dispersion of CCNs and the reduction in the content of hydrophilic groups in the blend generated complex micropaths for the water molecules, causing the water vapors to pass through the CCN-CS/CMC composite films through the extended path (Yu et al., 2022).

To evaluate the oxygen barrier (OB) capacity of the CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite films, the peroxide value (POV) of fresh soybean oil (SO) sealed using various films was determined and is depicted in Fig. S7f. The POV of SO sealed by the neat CS/CMC film was significantly higher than that of the CCN-CS/CMC films. This result indicated that the presence of CCNs enhanced the OB capacity of the nanocomposite film. This could be related to the denser internal cross-linked structure of the film due to the enhanced intermolecular interactions. The increase in compactness resulted in the generation of more complex pathways for oxygen molecule diffusion. A similar phenomenon was found by Zhai, Zhou, Zhang, Wang, & Hou (2022), who reported that the oxygen resistance of starch/PBAT films increased with increasing incorporation of Ag-ZnO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unique visible-light-driven antibacterial activity of the CCN-CS/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para: A packaging film with an active response and sustained antibacterial activity can effectively protect packaged food samples. The antibacterial activity of the produced CCN-CS/CMC films was tested with and without visible light irradiation against the representative bacteria S. aureus and E. coli. As indicated in Fig. 4 (a-b) and Fig. S8, the neat CS/CMC film treatment showed no inhibition of E. coli and S. aureus before and after visible light irradiation, suggesting that pure CS/CMC films possessed no visible-light-driven antibacterial activity. In addition, the survival rate change of E. coli and S. aureus for all film treatment groups could be neglected without visible light, indicating that all composite films had no antibacterial activity against both bacteria in the absence of visible light. In contrast, it was found that the survival rates of E. coli and S. aureus for the CCN-CS/CMC composite film treatment groups were significantly reduced when exposed to visible light. As the concentration of CCNs in the CS/CMC-based composite films increased from 0.10 mg/mL to 0.20 mg/mL, the visible-light-driven antibacterial performance of the composite films was improved significantly. The survival rates of E. coli and S. aureus for the CCN0.20-CS/CMC film treatment decreased to 0.04 % and 0.01 %, respectively. The antibacterial efficiency of the as-developed films is comparable to that of most reported films (Ni et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). Furthermore, inhibition zone measurements against E. coli and S. aureus with and without visible light irradiation were performed for all films; the results are shown in Fig. 4(c-d). Similar to the results of the spread plate experiment, the CCN-decorated composite films exhibited visible-light-driven antibacterial activity. The antibacterial activity of CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite films against gram-positive bacteria was better than that against gram-negative bacteria due to the different structures of bacteria; this result was consistent with previous reports (Ni et al., 2021). The above results indicated that the CCN0.20-CS/CMC films had excellent antibacterial effects on bacteria under visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible-light-driven antibacterial mechanism of the CCN-CS/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para:
The antibacterial mechanism of photocatalysts has been reported to be closely related to the generation of ROS (Jia, Song, Li, & Huang, 2019). Many studies have reported the development of antibacterial active packaging for fruit and vegetable preservation based on ROS generated by various materials, such as TiO2, ZnO and CuO (Duan, Li, Meng, Wang, & Wu, 2021; Zhai, Zhou, Zhang, Wang, & Hou, 2022; Bumbudsanpharoke, Harnkarnsujarit, Chongcharoenyanon, Kwon, & Ko, 2023; Hu et al., 2022). To gain insight into the visible-light-driven antibacterial mechanism of the CCN-CS/CMC composite films, various scavengers (O2·- for p-BQ, IPA for ·OH, EDTA-2Na for h+ and ASS for H2O2) were employed to identify the reactive species in the antibacterial process using a typical CCN0.20-CS/CMC film according to the literature (Ni, Wang, Sun, & Wang, 2022). Fig. 4e displays the inhibition zone diameter of the CCN0.20-CS/CMC film against E. coli and S. aureus in the presence of different scavengers. The diameter of the inhibition zone of the composite films against the two bacteria barely decreased after the addition of EDTA-2Na and ASS, implying that h+ and H2O2 were not present to contribute to photodynamic sterilization. The diameter of the inhibition zone only decreased slightly after the addition of IPA, suggesting that ·OH was probably not the main active species during the photodynamic inactivation of bacteria. In sharp contrast, the photodynamic antibacterial activity of the CCN0.20-CS/CMC film was greatly suppressed in the presence of p-BQ, indicating that O2·- played a critical role in the visible-light-driven antimicrobial process. Based on these results, the probable visible-light-driven antimicrobial mechanism of the CCN-CS/CMC composite films was proposed:
(1) g-C3N4 + hv → g-C3N4 (e− + h+)
(2) g-C3N4 (e−) + CPCDs → g-C3N4 + CPCDs (e−)
(3) g-C3N4 (e−) + O2 → g-C3N4 + O2
(4) CPCDs (e−) + O2 → CPCDs + O2
(5) Live bacteria + O2·- → Dead bacteria

Electronic spin resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was further applied to verify the generation of O2·- with DMPO as the spin probe (Fig. 4f). Under dark conditions, no EPR signals for DMPO-O2·- were observed in the presence of the CCN0.20-CS/CMC film. Under simulated visible light irradiation, the obvious characteristic signals of DMPO-O2·- appeared in the EPR spectrum, indicating O2·- production in the CCN-CS/CMC film. These results confirmed that O2·- was the main reactive component in visible-light-driven bacterial disinfection.

The outstanding photocatalytic antibacterial activity of the CCN-CS/CMC composite films under visible light could be attributed to the high utilization of light by the added CCNs and the efficient separation and transfer of photoexcited charge carriers in the CCNs (Ezati, Riahi, & Rhim, 2021). CCNs are believed to be metal-free photocatalysts with narrow band gap widths that possess excellent photocatalytic activity under visible light. This is mainly because the CPCDs in the CCNs can act as electron traps to capture photoexcited electrons generated on the photocatalyst surface, thus preventing the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers (Eqs. (1) and (2)). With the help of CPCDs, the photoinduced electrons and holes were effectively separated and transferred. The narrow band gap energy also enhanced the response of CCNs to visible light. The separated photoexcited electrons could further interact with the chemisorbed O2 to produce O2·- (Eqs. (3) and (4)). Therefore, the decoration of CCNs enabled the CCN-CS/CMC composite films to absorb visible light to generate a large number of reactive species (mainly O2·-) that then attacked pathogenic bacteria to inactivate them. According to previous studies, O2·- species can damage the cell membrane structure of microorganisms by oxidizing proteins and lipids; thus, the disinfection of pathogenic bacteria is achieved (Ezati, Riahi, & Rhim, 2021). Fig. 5
shows a schematic diagram of the visible-light-driven antimicrobial mechanism of the CCN-CS/CMC composite films.

The biological security of active substances added to food packaging is critical, especially in food applications. As shown in Fig. S9a, the relative cell viabilities of L929 cells treated with different CCN concentrations were above 98 % after coculturing for 24 h, reflecting the excellent biocompatibility of the CCNs. The impact of the CCNs on human red blood cells was analyzed by the hemolysis test (Fig. S9b). The results showed that CCNs with different concentrations had a relative hemolysis ratio of below 1.56 % (far below the permissible limit of 5 %), revealing their good blood compatibility (Zhang et al., 2022). As discussed above, the CCN-decorated CS/CMC blending films could be used safely in food preservation.

Bananas, a common perishable fruit, were used as an experimental model to evaluate the effectiveness of the CS/CMC-based films in extending food shelf life. The changes in the appearance, surface color, weight loss, and firmness of the bananas before and after storage are shown in Fig. 6(a-c) and Table S2. Fig. 6a shows that the unwrapped and PE-wrapped bananas began to brown and rot on the 4th day of storage, with these characteristics becoming more severe over time. A similar phenomenon was observed for the CS/CMC-wrapped bananas after six days of storage. Mixed bacterial infections of different degrees were observed at the top or bottom of the corresponding bananas. However, the bananas packed with the CCN0.20-CS/CMC composite film maintained a good appearance under the same storage conditions, and only black spots appeared on the surface after six days of storage. This confirmed the positive effect of the CCN-CS/CMC visible-light-driven antibacterial functional films on prolonging banana storage. As shown in Table S2, the decrease in the L* and b*-values of the banana surface color was lowest when the bananas were packaged with the CCN0.20-CS/CMC composite film, and the ΔE* value was the lowest (8.91 %). Moreover, significant weight loss of the bananas occurred in the unpacked group (control) during storage due to the moisture evaporation and respiration of the bananas themselves. In comparison, the weight loss of the bananas packed with PE films, CS/CMC films, and CCN0.20-CS/CMC films was significantly reduced, and CCN0.20-CS/CMC film-packed bananas exhibited the lowest weight loss (Fig. 6b). This phenomenon could be related to the excellent water resistance and barrier properties of the CCN0.20-CS/CMC composite film. Fig. 6c presents the hardness changes of the bananas before and after storage. There was no significant difference in the hardness of all banana samples before storage, but after eight days, the hardness of the bananas decreased to 6.02, 7.76, and 7.55 N for the control, PE film, and CS/CMC film treatment groups, respectively. However, the hardness of the bananas covered with the CCN0.20-CS/CMC composite film was 8.83 N, which was higher compared to those of the other treatment groups. Bananas packed with the CCN0.20-CS/CMC film had the lowest softening rate, which was associated with the protection of cell wall materials and the regulation of softening enzyme activity by the nanocomposite film (Ali et al., 2022). These results suggested that the prepared CCN0.20-CS/CMC visible-light-driven antibacterial composite film could continuously protect bananas from microbial infection and prolong their shelf life.

In summary, nonmetal CCNs with enhanced photocatalytic activity were successfully constructed and uniformly embedded in a natural CS/CMC blend biopolymer film matrix as photoactive nanofillers to fabricate controllable CCN-CS/CMC visible-light-driven antibacterial active nanocomposite films. Notably, no additional agents were introduced during the synthesis of the CCNs. CCN incorporation improved the thermal stability, mechanical strength, water resistance, and barrier mechanical properties of the CS/CMC films, depending on the CCN concentration. Furthermore, the functional packaging developed by combining the CCN-CS/CMC film with photodynamic antibacterial technology had an excellent antibacterial performance against typical foodborne pathogens (E. coli and S. aureus) under visible light. Radical-scavenging tests and EPR spectra confirmed that O2·- active species played a primary role in the visible-light-driven bacterial disinfection process of the CCN-CS/CMC film. Benefitting from the above features, the shelf life of bananas covered by the CCN0.20-CS/CMC film could be extended by more than four days compared to the control. The effects of the film on the respiration rate and physiological metabolism of bananas need further investigation. Moreover, cell viability and hemolysis experiments proved the biosafety and nontoxicity of the resultant CCNs. This study not only demonstrates that the CCN-CS/CMC nanocomposite film with visible-light-driven antibacterial function can be employed as a promising active packaging to reduce food spoilage but also affords a new avenue for broadening the application of photodynamic sterilization in the food field.
